content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you’ve shipped a web UI with meaningful in-progress state — a checkout, a multi-step form, a wizard, a drag-heavy board, an in-app editor, even a media player mid-scrub — and tested it only on desktop, there’s a bug you’ve probably never seen: on touchscreens (Chromebooks, Android tablets and phones), a horizontal swipe near the edge of the screen can be interpreted by the browser or OS as a “go back” gesture. One careless swipe and the user is kicked back a page, and whatever state they had is gone.
This post was drafted with AI assistance for research and structuring, then fact-checked, edited, and verified by me against real code and my own project.
This isn’t a checkout-specific problem. It’s a general hazard for any touchscreen UI that holds state the user would be upset to lose: an unsaved form, a multi-step flow, an in-progress upload, a game. I built a small repro + fix harness to test the available defenses properly instead of guessing — using a fake checkout flow as one concrete example, not the whole story.
Scope: this post covers Chromium-based browsers — Android Chrome (tested directly on a real phone) and desktop Chrome (tested via trackpad). ChromeOS/Chromebook shares the same Blink/Chromium engine as Android Chrome, so I’d expect the same results to hold there, but I haven’t personally verified it on a Chromebook yet — I’ve flagged that explicitly below rather than blur it with the results I’ve actually confirmed.
The four defense layers
These apply to any route or component you want to protect, not just a checkout page:

• overscroll-behavior-x: none — a CSS property that asks the browser to suppress horizontal overscroll-triggered navigation. Cheap, declarative, no JS required.

• touch-action: manipulation — restricts touch gesture handling to pan/pinch/tap, dropping the swipe-navigation gesture. Also CSS-only.

• A history stack guard — push a dummy history entry when the protected view mounts, and re-push on every popstate, so a single back-gesture pops the dummy entry instead of leaving the route. Framework-agnostic; works with any router or none at all.

• A router-level navigation blocker (e.g. React Router’s useBlocker) — intercepts in-app navigate()/<Link> transitions away from the protected view while it's active, with an explicit exemption for whatever transition legitimately ends the flow (checkout completing, form submitting, etc.).
The useful property of treating these as four independent toggles, rather than one bundled “fix,” is that you can isolate which layer is doing the actual work on a given platform/browser — because, as it turns out, it’s not the same layer everywhere.
A case study: a fake checkout flow
To test this concretely, I built a small demo: a 5-step fake checkout (Cart → Shipping → Payment → Review → Processing) with a progress bar and a “processing payment” indicator, all held in plain, unpersisted component state — so that if the page unmounts because the browser navigated away underneath it, the reset is visible immediately. The four layers above are wired as independent toggles (default all on) with an on-screen debug log timestamping every pushState, popstate, blocker decision, and route mount/unmount, since devtools console isn't reachable while you're mid-swipe on a phone.
Checkout is just the stand-in here — the same four layers, and the same testing caveats below, apply equally to a multi-step signup form, an onboarding wizard, or anything else with state worth protecting.
Full source, including the README with LAN-testing setup for real devices, is on GitHub: github.com/DeepCoomer/swipe-back-nav-demo.
Why automated testing (Playwright, DevTools emulation) can’t validate this
I used Playwright to verify the demo’s implementation — that toggles apply the right classes, the blocker fires and shows its confirm dialog, the debug log captures events, nothing throws in the console. That’s real and useful: it confirms the code is wired correctly. It is not evidence that any layer stops a real swipe-back gesture, and it would have been a mistake to publish results based on it alone.
The reason is architectural: Playwright drives the page via the DevTools Protocol, the same category of synthetic input as Chrome DevTools’ device-mode/touch emulation. Both simulate touch events for the page’s own JS. Neither reproduces the actual OS/browser-chrome edge-swipe-to-navigate gesture, because that gesture is recognized by the browser chrome or OS beneath the layer either tool operates at. You can flip every layer off in an automated test or in emulation and nothing will happen either way — there’s no gesture recognizer to trigger. The only way to know if a mitigation works is a real touchscreen device. Everything in the Results section below was verified that way — on a real Android phone and a real trackpad, not in emulation.
That also means the four layers don’t behave identically across platforms:

• Android Chrome : the actual target platform for overscroll-behavior-x and touch-action — they're Blink/Chromium features built specifically to suppress this gesture.

• Desktop trackpad three-finger swipe: a separate gesture path from touchscreen edge-swipe, worth testing on its own rather than assuming touchscreen results carry over — which is why it’s a separate row in the results below rather than an assumption.
Results
Android phone, Chrome (real device, edge-swipe gesture):

• All 4 layers OFF (baseline): swipe navigates away, checkout state resets — confirms the bug is real, not theoretical.

• Only overscroll-behavior-x ON: gesture fully suppressed — the swipe has no effect at all, no navigation attempt registers. The strongest single layer.

• Only touch-action ON: does not work — swipe still navigates away and state resets.

• Only the history guard ON: the swipe still triggers a real back-navigation attempt (popstate fires, visible in the debug log), but the guard's re-push happens fast enough that the net effect is the user stays put. This one is reactive rather than preventative — it lets the gesture happen and undoes it, rather than stopping it from starting.

• Only useBlocker ON: clicking the in-app "Home" link correctly triggers the Stay/Leave confirm dialog, and Stay keeps you on checkout — working exactly as designed for in-app navigation. The real swipe gesture told a different story: the dialog appears, but the swipe still ends up navigating to Home anyway. useBlocker reliably covers in-app navigation but is not, on its own, a reliable defense against the touchscreen gesture itself.

• All 4 layers ON (the shipped configuration): reliable — the gesture is stopped and checkout state is preserved.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

history stack guard in actionDesktop Chrome (real trackpad, 3-finger swipe, all 4 layers ON): blocked reliably. This was tested as the combined “shipped” configuration rather than isolating each layer individually, so I can’t yet say which single layer (if any) is carrying it on desktop the way overscroll-behavior-x clearly does on Android — worth a follow-up pass.
Chromebook (touchscreen), Chrome: I haven’t run this on a physical Chromebook yet — but ChromeOS shares Android Chrome’s Blink engine, so I’d bet on the same results holding. I’ll confirm and update this post once I get my hands on one.
Takeaways
No single layer was fully reliable alone on Android — that was the most useful (and mildly surprising) finding here. overscroll-behavior-x alone came closest, stopping the gesture outright. touch-action alone did nothing. The history guard alone worked, but reactively — it lets the browser attempt the navigation and then undoes it, rather than preventing it. And useBlocker alone reliably protects against in-app navigation (Link clicks, navigate() calls) but is not a reliable defense against the swipe gesture itself, since that gesture doesn't necessarily go through the same code path.
Practical recommendation: ship all four together. They cover different, only partially overlapping failure modes — CSS-level gesture suppression, a reactive history-stack backstop, and a separate guard against in-app navigation away from an active flow — and on Chromium, combining them held up reliably on both a real Android phone and a real desktop trackpad. This generalizes past checkout: any flow with in-progress state that would be genuinely bad to lose on a touchscreen — a form, a wizard, an upload, a game — is protected by the same four layers, not something checkout-specific.
Try it yourself: the full demo, including the toggleable defense layers and the on-screen debug log, is on GitHub — github.com/DeepCoomer/swipe-back-nav-demo.
The README covers running it exposed on your LAN so you can test on your own phone or Chromebook. If you get different results than what’s above, I’d genuinely like to hear about it.
